warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All 18 assertions are factually correct and faithful to the source. Names, ages, birthdates (computed from age via arithmetic), birthplaces, occupation, and residence are all accurately extracted. Evidence types are properly assigned: direct for explicitly stated facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation), indirect for computed birth years and inferred relationships. The conflict flag correctly identifies the Ireland vs. Pennsylvania birthplace discrepancy. No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: All individuals and facts from the census record were extracted: Thomas Flynn (6 assertions covering name, age, birth_year, birthplace, occupation, residence), Mary Flynn (6 assertions: name, age, birth_year, birthplace, residence, relationship), and Patrick Flynn (6 assertions: name, age, birth_year, birthplace, residence, relationship). The skill correctly did NOT fabricate an occupation for Mary (census lists only Thomas). All required data captured.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls passed correct arguments. Log entry recorded proper metadata (record type, county, state, household head, year, outcome, notes). Tree edit successfully added the source with correct title and author. Research append correctly structured 19 operations (1 source + 18 assertions) with proper schema, IDs, and content. No tool calls failed or returned fixture_not_found errors.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Separate assertions for Thomas: name (a_001), age (a_002), birth_year (a_003), birthplace (a_004), occupation (a_005), residence (a_006). Mary and Patrick similarly decomposed. Compound information like 'age 32 at 1850' is not crammed into a single assertion—instead age and computed birth_year are distinct entries with appropriate fact_types and evidence classifications. Downstream skills can query individual facts cleanly.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants are correctly identified and distinguished from the recorder. For name, age, birthplace, occupation: informant is 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with proximity 'household_member'—someone in the household must have supplied this info. For residence and relationship: informant is 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'—the enumerator physically visited and observed. The skill correctly reasoned that Patrick (age 5) could not self-report, so a parent must have provided his information. Proximity values are from the permitted enum and correctly applied.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, occupation (explicitly stated in record); residence (enumerator witnessed location). Indirect evidence: birth_year (computed from stated age via arithmetic, not directly recorded); relationships (Mary as spouse and Patrick as child inferred from household position, since 1850 census had no explicit relationship column). This distinction is critical for conflict resolution and is applied consistently across all three household members.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly extracted 17 assertions for Thomas, Bridget, and John, created person stubs I5 and I6 with proper gender and preferred names, and added ParentChild relationships R4 and R5. However, the skill created a stub for Bridget when Mary (I3) already exists in the tree with matching gender (Female), and created a stub for John when James (I4) already exists with matching gender (Male). The enumeration checklist incorrectly treated name mismatch alone as a signal to create new stubs, violating the requirement that 'Mary and James should not be recreated' per the test context. This represents a critical logic error: existing siblings should be recognized by relationship and gender match, not exact name match.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for Thomas (5 assertions), Bridget (6 assertions), and John (6 assertions), meeting the requirement to extract per-person assertions for every household member listed in the census. However, it failed to recognize and skip the existing person stubs (Mary/I3 and James/I4) that were already in the tree. The test explicitly required detection that Mary and James already exist and that 'the enumeration must catch this and skip them.' Instead, the skill created duplicate stubs, omitting the required skip logic.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The tree_edit operations for adding persons (Bridget and John as I5 and I6) are structurally correct: no id field is passed, gender and preferred birth names are properly formatted. The ParentChild relationships correctly reference I2 (Thomas) as parent and I5, I6 as children. The research_append calls pass 17 well-formed assertion entries with proper source_id, record_id, fact_type, informant proximity, and evidence_type fields. However, the logical error in the enumeration checklist (not recognizing existing Mary/James stubs) means the tool calls created persons that should not have been created, making the overall argument strategy incorrect even though the individual call shapes are valid.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each of the 17 assertions is atomic: name facts are separate from age/birth/birthplace/relationship/residence facts. For example, Bridget's entry includes separate assertions for name (a_019), age (a_020), birth year (a_021), birthplace (a_022), residence (a_023), and relationship (a_024). No compound facts are merged into a single value field; each fact_type carries one extractable piece of information.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill generally identifies informants well: household members (parents/head) for name/age/birthplace facts with proximity 'household_member,' and census enumerator for residence with proximity 'witness.' However, the relationship assertions for Bridget and John (a_024, a_030) both state 'Inferred from household structure — no explicit informant for relationships in 1850 census' with proximity 'unknown,' which is correct per the rubric guidance that unknown is appropriate when relationships are inferred from structure in pre-1880 censuses. The skill correctly avoids listing the enumerator as informant for birth/age facts. Overall identification is strong, though the informant_bias_notes could be slightly more concise.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All evidence_type assignments are correct: direct for stated facts (names, ages, birthplaces, occupations as they appear in columns), indirect for computed birth years derived from age arithmetic (e.g., ~1843 from age 7), indirect for the relationship assertion inferred from household position in a pre-1880 census (no relationship column). The skill properly distinguishes stated age ('age 7' = direct) from inferred birth year ('~1843' = indirect) and correctly marks the 1850 census relationship as indirect per the rubric's guidance on pre-1880 co-residence relationships.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly extracted 17 assertions for Thomas, Bridget, and John, created person stubs I5 and I6 with proper gender and preferred names, and added ParentChild relationships R4 and R5. However, the skill created a stub for Bridget when Mary (I3) already exists in the tree with matching gender (Female), and created a stub for John when James (I4) already exists with matching gender (Male). The enumeration checklist incorrectly treated name mismatch alone as a signal to create new stubs, violating the requirement that 'Mary and James should not be recreated' per the test context. This represents a critical logic error: existing siblings should be recognized by relationship and gender match, not exact name match.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for Thomas (5 assertions), Bridget (6 assertions), and John (6 assertions), meeting the requirement to extract per-person assertions for every household member listed in the census. However, it failed to recognize and skip the existing person stubs (Mary/I3 and James/I4) that were already in the tree. The test explicitly required detection that Mary and James already exist and that 'the enumeration must catch this and skip them.' Instead, the skill created duplicate stubs, omitting the required skip logic.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The tree_edit operations for adding persons (Bridget and John as I5 and I6) are structurally correct: no id field is passed, gender and preferred birth names are properly formatted. The ParentChild relationships correctly reference I2 (Thomas) as parent and I5, I6 as children. The research_append calls pass 17 well-formed assertion entries with proper source_id, record_id, fact_type, informant proximity, and evidence_type fields. However, the logical error in the enumeration checklist (not recognizing existing Mary/James stubs) means the tool calls created persons that should not have been created, making the overall argument strategy incorrect even though the individual call shapes are valid.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the 17 assertions is atomic: name facts are separate from age/birth/birthplace/relationship/residence facts. For example, Bridget's entry includes separate assertions for name (a_019), age (a_020), birth year (a_021), birthplace (a_022), residence (a_023), and relationship (a_024). No compound facts are merged into a single value field; each fact_type carries one extractable piece of information.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill generally identifies informants well: household members (parents/head) for name/age/birthplace facts with proximity 'household_member,' and census enumerator for residence with proximity 'witness.' However, the relationship assertions for Bridget and John (a_024, a_030) both state 'Inferred from household structure — no explicit informant for relationships in 1850 census' with proximity 'unknown,' which is correct per the rubric guidance that unknown is appropriate when relationships are inferred from structure in pre-1880 censuses. The skill correctly avoids listing the enumerator as informant for birth/age facts. Overall identification is strong, though the informant_bias_notes could be slightly more concise.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All evidence_type assignments are correct: direct for stated facts (names, ages, birthplaces, occupations as they appear in columns), indirect for computed birth years derived from age arithmetic (e.g., ~1843 from age 7), indirect for the relationship assertion inferred from household position in a pre-1880 census (no relationship column). The skill properly distinguishes stated age ('age 7' = direct) from inferred birth year ('~1843' = indirect) and correctly marks the 1850 census relationship as indirect per the rubric's guidance on pre-1880 co-residence relationships.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most assertions are factually accurate and properly sourced from the death certificate. However, there is one significant issue: name and sex assertions incorrectly assign Mary Flynn (wife) as informant with 'witness' proximity for facts she reported about the deceased. Per the instructions, Mary Flynn should be identified as the actual informant for name/sex/race/occupation/age facts (which she reported), but her proximity should be 'household_member' or similar, not 'witness' (she didn't witness his birth or original identity). Additionally, death_date and death_place are attributed to Dr. Stein, but Mary Flynn as the wife likely reported the death location and timing to the physician; the physician certified the cause and duration, not necessarily the date/place. The attribution conflates the recorder (certificate creator) with the actual informants (Mary Flynn for death facts, physician for medical facts).

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all facts present on the death certificate: name, sex, race, death date, death place, age, occupation, birthplace, parents' names and birthplaces, cause of death, illness duration, burial date/place, informant identity and residence. Decomposition into atomic assertions (separate assertions for death date vs. death place, age vs. birth year) is appropriate. No required facts from the certificate were omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls executed successfully with matched.kind='live'. The research_log_append call correctly captured the user-provided record context. The tree_edit call correctly added the source description S1. The research_append call correctly batched all 20 assertions and the source entry with proper schema. No calls landed in fixture_not_found; arguments matched the expected shapes semantically.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion captures a single, extractable fact. Death date and death place are separate assertions (not bundled). Age and computed birth year are separate assertions. Name, sex, race, occupation are each their own assertions. Parents' names and birthplaces are atomic. Relationship and residence for the informant are separated. No compound claims within individual value fields.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified Mary Flynn (wife) as the informant for most facts she reported, and Dr. Stein for medical facts, and J.J. Franey for burial facts. However, informant_proximity assignments are problematic: (1) Facts about the deceased (name, sex, race, occupation, age) correctly identify Mary Flynn as informant but assign 'witness' proximity—she reported these as a household member, not as a witness to his birth or original identity. The proximity should reflect she's reporting on a household member she knows, not witnessing an event. (2) The birth_year computed assertion correctly uses 'unknown' proximity. (3) Informant proximity on death_date and death_place assigned to 'official_duty' for Dr. Stein, but the physician likely recorded timing information provided by Mary Flynn, not independently certified the exact date/place; the proximity classification obscures this. The rubric allows 'household_member' which would be more accurate for Mary Flynn's role in reporting decedent facts.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned across all assertions. Death facts (date, place, cause, illness duration) attributed to the physician are marked 'direct' (he was present and certified them, or they were explicitly stated). Birth facts (place, parents' names and birthplaces) are marked 'indirect' with rationales explaining Mary Flynn was not present at the birth in Ireland and is relaying secondhand knowledge. The computed birth_year is marked 'indirect' with explanation of the analyst computation. Living informant facts (Mary Flynn's name, relationship, residence) are marked 'direct' as self-reported. The classification correctly reflects whether the informant witnessed/participated in the fact or was relaying secondhand information.

**Planning the extraction:**
- Source type: **derivative** (death cert records what informants stated)
- Three informant streams: Mary Flynn (wife) for identity/family facts, Dr. Stein for medical/death facts, J.J. Franey (undertaker) for burial facts
- 20 assertions across `deceased` and `informant` roles
- Source will be `src_001` / `S1`

No sibling stubs needed — the subject (`I1`) is the `deceased`, not a `child_N`.

---

**Assertions:** 20 total
- `deceased` (17): name, sex, race, death date, death place, age, birth year (computed ~1845), occupation, birth place, father name, father birthplace, mother maiden name, mother birthplace, cause of death, illness duration, burial date, burial place
- `informant` (3): Mary Flynn — name, relationship (wife), residence (214 Main St, Shenandoah PA)

**Tree changes:** `S1` source description added; no new persons (subject is existing `I1`)

**Key classification notes:**
- Death date/place & medical facts → Dr. Stein, `official_duty`, `primary`, `direct`
- Burial facts → J.J. Franey, `official_duty`, `primary`, `direct`
- Decedent's birth place, parents' names & birthplaces → Mary Flynn (wife), `family_not_present`, `secondary`, **`indirect`** (she wasn't present at birth in Ireland)
- Computed birth year `~1845` → analyst inference, `unknown`, `indirect`

**Next steps:** Run `check-warnings` for plausibility checks, then `assertion-classification` to refine the GPS evidence classifications, or `person-evidence` to link `a_001`–`a_020` to `I1`.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Most assertions are factually accurate and properly sourced from the death certificate. However, there is one significant issue: name and sex assertions incorrectly assign Mary Flynn (wife) as informant with 'witness' proximity for facts she reported about the deceased. Per the instructions, Mary Flynn should be identified as the actual informant for name/sex/race/occupation/age facts (which she reported), but her proximity should be 'household_member' or similar, not 'witness' (she didn't witness his birth or original identity). Additionally, death_date and death_place are attributed to Dr. Stein, but Mary Flynn as the wife likely reported the death location and timing to the physician; the physician certified the cause and duration, not necessarily the date/place. The attribution conflates the recorder (certificate creator) with the actual informants (Mary Flynn for death facts, physician for medical facts).

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all facts present on the death certificate: name, sex, race, death date, death place, age, occupation, birthplace, parents' names and birthplaces, cause of death, illness duration, burial date/place, informant identity and residence. Decomposition into atomic assertions (separate assertions for death date vs. death place, age vs. birth year) is appropriate. No required facts from the certificate were omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls executed successfully with matched.kind='live'. The research_log_append call correctly captured the user-provided record context. The tree_edit call correctly added the source description S1. The research_append call correctly batched all 20 assertions and the source entry with proper schema. No calls landed in fixture_not_found; arguments matched the expected shapes semantically.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion captures a single, extractable fact. Death date and death place are separate assertions (not bundled). Age and computed birth year are separate assertions. Name, sex, race, occupation are each their own assertions. Parents' names and birthplaces are atomic. Relationship and residence for the informant are separated. No compound claims within individual value fields.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified Mary Flynn (wife) as the informant for most facts she reported, and Dr. Stein for medical facts, and J.J. Franey for burial facts. However, informant_proximity assignments are problematic: (1) Facts about the deceased (name, sex, race, occupation, age) correctly identify Mary Flynn as informant but assign 'witness' proximity—she reported these as a household member, not as a witness to his birth or original identity. The proximity should reflect she's reporting on a household member she knows, not witnessing an event. (2) The birth_year computed assertion correctly uses 'unknown' proximity. (3) Informant proximity on death_date and death_place assigned to 'official_duty' for Dr. Stein, but the physician likely recorded timing information provided by Mary Flynn, not independently certified the exact date/place; the proximity classification obscures this. The rubric allows 'household_member' which would be more accurate for Mary Flynn's role in reporting decedent facts.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned across all assertions. Death facts (date, place, cause, illness duration) attributed to the physician are marked 'direct' (he was present and certified them, or they were explicitly stated). Birth facts (place, parents' names and birthplaces) are marked 'indirect' with rationales explaining Mary Flynn was not present at the birth in Ireland and is relaying secondhand knowledge. The computed birth_year is marked 'indirect' with explanation of the analyst computation. Living informant facts (Mary Flynn's name, relationship, residence) are marked 'direct' as self-reported. The classification correctly reflects whether the informant witnessed/participated in the fact or was relaying secondhand information.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertions are factually accurate and properly grounded in the source record. The skill correctly extracted Patrick Flynn (groom), Catherine Gallagher (bride), all their stated facts (ages, birthplaces, residence, occupation, parents), and the marriage event (date, place, officiant). The distinction between direct evidence (stated facts like age 25) and indirect evidence (computed birth year ~1845) is properly applied. The birthplace conflict (Ireland vs. Pennsylvania objective) is correctly flagged for resolution, not ignored. No fabricated material.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for both primary parties (groom and bride) as required, covering all extractable facts: names, ages, birthplaces, residences, occupations, parents' names, and the marriage event itself. Parent identities (Thomas Flynn, Mary Brennan, James Gallagher, Bridget Dolan) are fully captured. Witnesses are noted as FAN associates with identifying facts only, per instructions. No major portions of the record were missed.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three tool calls executed successfully with no fixture_not_found errors (all matched.kind == 'live'). research_log_append correctly documented the user-supplied record with appropriate query structure. tree_edit correctly added the source with proper metadata. research_append submitted 21 assertions in valid batches with correct schema structure (section/op fields, proper entry formatting). Arguments were semantically appropriate for each operation.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Names, ages, birthplaces, residences, occupations, and relationships are all decomposed into separate entries with distinct fact_type values. Compound information (e.g., 'Patrick Flynn, age 25, born Ireland') has been properly split into atomic assertions (a_001 name, a_002 age, a_004 birthplace, a_003 computed birth year). No assertions conflate multiple facts into a single value.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants are correctly and specifically identified. Patrick Flynn is identified as informant for his own facts (age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parents) with informant_proximity 'self'. Catherine Gallagher is similarly identified for her facts with 'self' proximity. Rev. Daniel O'Connor (officiant) is correctly assigned as informant for the marriage event itself with informant_proximity 'official_duty', properly distinguishing the recorder (clerk) from the actual informant. Parent names (Thomas Flynn, Mary Brennan, etc.) correctly use 'self' proximity with the reporting party (groom/bride) as informant.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned throughout. Stated facts (age 25, name Patrick Flynn, birthplace Ireland, occupation coal miner, parents' names) are marked 'direct' because they were explicitly stated on the marriage license by the informants. Birth years computed from stated ages (~1845, ~1848) are correctly marked 'indirect' with clear rationale that they are derived by arithmetic, not stated facts. Marriage date and place are marked 'direct' as explicitly stated. No misclassifications that would undermine downstream conflict resolution or fact evaluation.

Names, ages, birth years (estimated from age at marriage), birthplaces, residences, and parent relationships are correctly extracted with proper evidence types (direct for stated facts, indirect for inferred birth years). Marriage event correctly sourced to civil registrar. No fabricated or unsupported claims.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Skill extracted all required facts: both spouses' names, ages, birthplaces, residences, marriage event (date, place), and all parent relationships (João Ramos da Silva, Maria da Conceição for groom; Miguel Florencio, Josefa de Araujo for bride). Four parent-name assertions also created. ARK was properly sent to image_read. Nothing required by the input state was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: ARK '3:1:3QS7-99QG-KBTG' passed correctly to image_read's ark parameter, matching expected substring '~3QS7-99QG-KBTG'. research_log_append, tree_edit, and research_append calls all used correct projectPath and valid operation/section syntax. No fixture_not_found errors. All tool calls were semantically appropriate.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion isolates a single fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, and marriage facts are separate entries. Parent relationships are decomposed into distinct relationship assertions plus separate parent-name entries. No compound values in fact_type fields; structured_value and supporting fields (date, place) do not conflate multiple facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Groom and bride facts correctly identify the person reporting (self, informant_proximity='self'). Parent-name facts identify groom/bride as the informant with self proximity, appropriate because they reported their parents' names on the marriage record. Marriage event correctly names 'civil registrar' with informant_proximity='official_duty'. Proximity values are specific and accurate to the source context.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Direct evidence assigned correctly: stated name, age, birthplace, residence, parent names, and marriage (all explicitly recorded on the civil registration). Birth year correctly marked indirect with explanatory bias_notes (inferred by subtracting age from marriage year; 1930–1931 range noted for groom, 1935–1936 for bride). Relationship assertions to parents marked direct (explicitly stated on record). All classifications align with source content.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All extracted assertions factually match the provided GedcomX record. Birth year ~1845 correctly inferred from age. Birthplace Ireland, residence Branch Township/Schuylkill/Pennsylvania all match source facts. Relationship inferred correctly from household position (1850 census, no explicit relationship column). Record match check correctly reported MXHY-TP4 already accepted to tree person LZNY-BRF. Log entry and source citations are accurate. No fabrications or unsupported claims.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill extracted all available facts from the record: Patrick Flynn's name, birth year, birthplace, residence, and relationship to household head; Thomas Flynn's name, residence, and reciprocal relationship. Successfully called record_person_matches with correct ID (MXHY-TP4) and reported the match result. Wrote source, log entry, and all 8 assertions to research.json. Per-test context requirement to extract assertions despite match status was met—extraction was not replaced by match check.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: record_person_matches called with id='MXHY-TP4' (exact match to expected_args). research_log_append and tree_edit called with correct projectPath and valid operation/tool arguments. research_append batch corrections (after first validation failure) properly changed record_persona_id to null per specification when log entry had no resultsRef. All MCP calls matched expected behavior or reasonable adaptive corrections.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion contains a single extractable fact. Birth year (~1845) is atomic and separate from birthplace (Ireland), which is separate from residence. Relationship assertions decomposed cleanly: Patrick's relationship to Thomas as 'child_inferred' is one assertion; Thomas's reciprocal relationship as 'parent_inferred' is a separate assertion. No compound values crammed into single fact_type entries. Facts are independently queryable by downstream skills.

aggregated_dimensions.rationale: For child's name/age/birthplace (P1): correctly identified 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and explained reasoning ('child ~5 could not self-report; parent likely reported'). For Thomas Flynn (head): 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with proximity 'household_member'. For residence facts (both persons): 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness' (enumerator was the recorder/observer). Informant_bias_notes explain the reasoning. Proximity values are correct enum values.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Stated facts (name, birthplace, residence) marked direct—correct. Birth year computed from age in 1850 census marked indirect—correct (age was stated; year derived from it). Relationships from 1850 census (no explicit relationship column) marked indirect—correct per rubric ('relationship inferred from household position in 1850/1860 is indirect'). Direct vs. indirect assignments align with whether facts were explicitly stated vs. inferred from context. No mis-assignments affecting conflict resolution trust.

output.text_response:
Reading both files up front — research.json and tree.gedcomx.json — already done. Now I'll plan the extraction before writing.

**Record analysis:**
- Source type: original (1850 U.S. Census schedule)
- Personas: P1 = Patrick Flynn (`child_1`), P2 = Thomas Flynn (`head_of_household`)
- No sources, assertions, or log entries exist yet → new `src_001`, new `S` entry, new log entry needed
- **5d sibling check:** P1 is `child_1` ✓ but Thomas Flynn (P2) is NOT in `tree.gedcomx.json` — only I1 (Patrick Flynn) exists → **skip stubs**, no parent terminus available
- No siblings listed in this record anyway

**Assertions planned (8 total):**
- P1: name, birth-year (indirect, computed from age), birthplace, residence, relationship-to-parent
- P2: name, residence, relationship-to-child

Firing log entry + tree source in parallel (independent):Log `log_001`, source `S1` both confirmed. Now firing the batched `research_append` — source + 8 assertions in one call:The validator requires that assertions with `record_persona_id` have a sidecar (`results_ref`), but `log_001` was logged without one (no `stagedResultsRef` was available from the handoff). Per the skill spec, when there's no staged sidecar, I must use `record_persona_id: null` — treating this as equivalent to a `record_read`-sourced record. Retrying:All 9 writes confirmed. Now checking whether MXHY-TP4 is already attached to a tree person on FamilySearch:All done. Here's the terse summary:

---

**Extraction complete — 1850 U.S. Census, Patrick Flynn (MXHY-TP4)**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — original, FamilySearch, NARA M432
- **Log:** `log_001` (no staged sidecar available from handoff → `record_persona_id: null` on all assertions; downstream `same_person` matching must proceed via name/place rather than persona ID)
- **Assertions:** 8 total
  - `child_1` (Patrick Flynn, P1): name, birth-year ~1845 *(indirect — computed from age)*, birthplace Ireland, residence 1850, relationship-inferred
  - `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn, P2): name, residence 1850, relationship-inferred
- **Tree changes:** `S1` source added; no sibling stubs (Thomas Flynn not yet in tree, trigger condition not met)
- **Key finding:** Birthplace **Ireland** — Patrick was born in Ireland, not Pennsylvania, contradicting the project objective's "born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania"
- **Match check:** MXHY-TP4 is **already accepted** on FamilySearch tree person **LZNY-BRF** (Patrick Flynn, confidence 5 / score 0.97, accepted 2024-03-15) — the local subject I1 should be linked

**Next steps:** Run `check-warnings` for plausibility checks, then `person-evidence` to evaluate whether I1 = the LZNY-BRF tree person, and whether Thomas Flynn should be added to the tree as a candidate parent.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All extracted assertions factually match the provided GedcomX record. Birth year ~1845 correctly inferred from age. Birthplace Ireland, residence Branch Township/Schuylkill/Pennsylvania all match source facts. Relationship inferred correctly from household position (1850 census, no explicit relationship column). Record match check correctly reported MXHY-TP4 already accepted to tree person LZNY-BRF. Log entry and source citations are accurate. No fabrications or unsupported claims.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Skill extracted all available facts from the record: Patrick Flynn's name, birth year, birthplace, residence, and relationship to household head; Thomas Flynn's name, residence, and reciprocal relationship. Successfully called record_person_matches with correct ID (MXHY-TP4) and reported the match result. Wrote source, log entry, and all 8 assertions to research.json. Per-test context requirement to extract assertions despite match status was met—extraction was not replaced by match check.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: record_person_matches called with id='MXHY-TP4' (exact match to expected_args). research_log_append and tree_edit called with correct projectPath and valid operation/tool arguments. research_append batch corrections (after first validation failure) properly changed record_persona_id to null per specification when log entry had no resultsRef. All MCP calls matched expected behavior or reasonable adaptive corrections.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion contains a single extractable fact. Birth year (~1845) is atomic and separate from birthplace (Ireland), which is separate from residence. Relationship assertions decomposed cleanly: Patrick's relationship to Thomas as 'child_inferred' is one assertion; Thomas's reciprocal relationship as 'parent_inferred' is a separate assertion. No compound values crammed into single fact_type entries. Facts are independently queryable by downstream skills.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: For child's name/age/birthplace (P1): correctly identified 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and explained reasoning ('child ~5 could not self-report; parent likely reported'). For Thomas Flynn (head): 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with proximity 'household_member'. For residence facts (both persons): 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness' (enumerator was the recorder/observer). Informant_bias_notes explain the reasoning. Proximity values are correct enum values.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Stated facts (name, birthplace, residence) marked direct—correct. Birth year computed from age in 1850 census marked indirect—correct (age was stated; year derived from it). Relationships from 1850 census (no explicit relationship column) marked indirect—correct per rubric ('relationship inferred from household position in 1850/1860 is indirect'). Direct vs. indirect assignments align with whether facts were explicitly stated vs. inferred from context. No mis-assignments affecting conflict resolution trust.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted and persisted assertions from the FamilySearch 1850 census record for Patrick Flynn and Thomas Flynn, with accurate evidence-type classification (birth year as indirect, birthplace as direct, relationship as indirect). However, there are two unsupported assertions: the birth date fact was labeled as indirect evidence in the assertion definition, but the raw fact_type 'birth' with value 'Ireland' created ambiguity—the skill treated a place value as a separate birth assertion rather than clarifying it was birthplace. Additionally, the skill asserted Thomas Flynn's name without strong evidence that he is the actual father (relationship is indexed by FamilySearch, not explicit in the 1850 census structure itself), though this is clearly flagged in the notes.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all required extractions: name, birth date (~1845 calculated from age), birthplace (Ireland), residence (1850 Branch Township Schuylkill PA), and the ParentChild relationship. Created appropriate source entry pointing to the 1850 census, appended a research log entry, and produced 7 atomized assertions. No required work was omitted; the skill completed the full task from record read through assertion persistence.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed correct arguments. record_read was called with the correct ARK (68Q9-K34P, matching ~68Q9-K34P expected). tree_edit was invoked correctly with add_source operation. research_log_append provided accurate recordId and outcome. research_append persisted all 7 assertions with complete structured metadata. No tool calls landed in fixture_not_found; all arguments semantically matched expectations.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Patrick Flynn has separate entries for name (a_001), birth year (a_002), birthplace (a_003), residence (a_004), and relationship (a_005). Thomas Flynn has separate entries for name (a_006) and residence (a_007). Compound information (e.g., 'born 1845 in Ireland') was decomposed into distinct assertions with separate fact_type and value fields. Downstream skills can query individual facts without parsing compound values.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants and assessed proximity. For Patrick's name, birthplace, and birth year, the informant is 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member', reasoning that a 5-year-old could not self-report. For Patrick's and Thomas's residence, the informant is 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness', reflecting direct observation. For Thomas's name, informant is 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with proximity 'household_member'. Each proximity value correctly distinguishes the recorder from the actual informant and matches the closed enum set.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are assigned correctly. Patrick's birthplace 'Ireland' is direct (explicitly stated in the record). Patrick's birth year ~1845 is indirect (computed from stated age ca. 5 in 1850, not directly stated as a date). The ParentChild relationship is correctly labeled indirect because the 1850 census has no explicit relationship column—the relationship is inferred from household position and FamilySearch's index, not stated on the original document. Patrick's and Thomas's names and residence are direct (stated or observed in the record). The skill's rationale explicitly notes the pre-1880 census policy on relationships, demonstrating correct application of the rubric standard.

Good. Now let me read the project files and fetch the record simultaneously.Good. I have both project files and the record data. Let me analyze:

**Record:** 1850 U.S. Census — Patrick Flynn, child_1; Thomas Flynn, head_of_household (parent)
- Patrick: born 1845, Ireland; residing Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA
- Thomas: residing Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA; indexed as PatrickCh's parent
- No siblings listed; no relationship column (1850 census → indirect)
- Tree: only I1 (Patrick) exists; Thomas not in tree → sibling stub trigger skipped

**Sibling check:** No other children in record → no stubs needed.

Now firing tree_edit (add_source) and research_log_append in parallel:S1 and log_001 confirmed. Now persisting source + 7 assertions in one batch (src_001 predicted — sources was empty):All written and validated. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — derivative (FamilySearch indexed record), 1850 U.S. Census, Branch Township, Schuylkill Co., PA

**Assertions:** 7 total
- `child_1` (Patrick Flynn) — a_001–a_005: name, birth year (indirect/calculated), birthplace, residence, relationship
- `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn) — a_006–a_007: name, residence

**Tree changes:** S1 source added; no person stubs (Thomas Flynn not in tree; no siblings in record)

**Key findings:**
- ⚠️ **Birthplace conflict:** record states Patrick born in **Ireland**; project objective says born in Pennsylvania — warrants conflict-resolution
- Relationship (Patrick → child of Thomas) is **indirect** (pre-1880 census; no relationship column)
- Birth year 1845 is **calculated** from age, not stated directly

**Next:** `check-warnings` for plausibility flags, then `person-evidence` to link these assertions to I1 (Patrick) and create a Thomas Flynn candidate.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted and persisted assertions from the FamilySearch 1850 census record for Patrick Flynn and Thomas Flynn, with accurate evidence-type classification (birth year as indirect, birthplace as direct, relationship as indirect). However, there are two unsupported assertions: the birth date fact was labeled as indirect evidence in the assertion definition, but the raw fact_type 'birth' with value 'Ireland' created ambiguity—the skill treated a place value as a separate birth assertion rather than clarifying it was birthplace. Additionally, the skill asserted Thomas Flynn's name without strong evidence that he is the actual father (relationship is indexed by FamilySearch, not explicit in the 1850 census structure itself), though this is clearly flagged in the notes.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all required extractions: name, birth date (~1845 calculated from age), birthplace (Ireland), residence (1850 Branch Township Schuylkill PA), and the ParentChild relationship. Created appropriate source entry pointing to the 1850 census, appended a research log entry, and produced 7 atomized assertions. No required work was omitted; the skill completed the full task from record read through assertion persistence.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed correct arguments. record_read was called with the correct ARK (68Q9-K34P, matching ~68Q9-K34P expected). tree_edit was invoked correctly with add_source operation. research_log_append provided accurate recordId and outcome. research_append persisted all 7 assertions with complete structured metadata. No tool calls landed in fixture_not_found; all arguments semantically matched expectations.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Patrick Flynn has separate entries for name (a_001), birth year (a_002), birthplace (a_003), residence (a_004), and relationship (a_005). Thomas Flynn has separate entries for name (a_006) and residence (a_007). Compound information (e.g., 'born 1845 in Ireland') was decomposed into distinct assertions with separate fact_type and value fields. Downstream skills can query individual facts without parsing compound values.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants and assessed proximity. For Patrick's name, birthplace, and birth year, the informant is 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member', reasoning that a 5-year-old could not self-report. For Patrick's and Thomas's residence, the informant is 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness', reflecting direct observation. For Thomas's name, informant is 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with proximity 'household_member'. Each proximity value correctly distinguishes the recorder from the actual informant and matches the closed enum set.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are assigned correctly. Patrick's birthplace 'Ireland' is direct (explicitly stated in the record). Patrick's birth year ~1845 is indirect (computed from stated age ca. 5 in 1850, not directly stated as a date). The ParentChild relationship is correctly labeled indirect because the 1850 census has no explicit relationship column—the relationship is inferred from household position and FamilySearch's index, not stated on the original document. Patrick's and Thomas's names and residence are direct (stated or observed in the record). The skill's rationale explicitly notes the pre-1880 census policy on relationships, demonstrating correct application of the rubric standard.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The extraction and matching logic are factually sound, but the skill failed to persist all assertions to the tree on the first attempt due to a schema validation error on record_persona_id. The final retry succeeded, but the error reveals a conceptual inconsistency: the skill first set record_persona_id to P1 (correct for a user_provided log), then discovered the validator required null when no sidecar exists. The second batch correctly nullified all persona IDs and persisted successfully. The assertion content itself is accurate—informants are correctly identified (household member for name/age/birthplace, enumerator for residence), evidence types properly distinguish direct vs. indirect (birth year correctly marked indirect), and the relationship is appropriately inferred for an 1850 census. The record_record_matches call correctly identified both pending matches (1860 census confidence 4, death certificate confidence 3) and highlighted the higher-priority 1860 lead. No fabricated material; the gap about lacking county/parish-level birthplace is acknowledged.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: extracted six assertions (Patrick's name, birth year ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence 1850, child-of-Thomas relationship, and Thomas's name), called record_record_matches for MXHY-TP4 as instructed, reported both pending collateral matches with confidence scores, highlighted the 1860 census as the higher-priority follow-up, and added the source to the tree. The log entry was created, assertions were persisted to research.json after the retry, and the FamilySearch matches were fully documented with confidence and score details. No silent omissions of required work.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The record_record_matches call was correctly formed with id='MXHY-TP4' and matched the expected predicate. However, the initial research_append call (call #3) failed validation because all assertions carried record_persona_id='P1' despite the log entry having no sidecar (results_ref=null). The validator correctly rejected this. The retry (call #6) corrected the error by setting all record_persona_id values to null, which passed validation. This is a partial: the logical args were sound (sections, ops, entry structure), but the first attempt mishandled the schema constraint. The retry demonstrates correction, not a fundamental misunderstanding, but the initial call was indeed wrong.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each of the seven assertions (six for Patrick, one for Thomas) is atomic: a_001 name only, a_002 birth year only (derived, separate from the stated age that is not extracted), a_003 birthplace only, a_004 residence only, a_005 relationship only, a_006 Thomas's name only, a_007 (in final batch) Thomas's name again. No compound facts crammed into single values. Birth year ~1845 is correctly separated from the age-derivation note (which appears in informant_bias_notes, not conflated into value). The relationship assertion decomposes position in household (the observable fact) from the inference (child of Thomas) via structured_value and fact_type, not blended.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: For Patrick's name, age, and birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with informant_proximity='household_member' is accurate and specific. A ~5-year-old could not self-report; a parent must have supplied these facts. Informant_bias_notes explain the reasoning. For residence: 'census enumerator' with informant_proximity='witness' is correct—the enumerator observed where the household lived. For Thomas's name: 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with proximity='household_member' appropriately captures that an adult likely self-reported or was reported by a spouse, without over-specifying. All informant_proximity values are from the closed enum (self, witness, household_member, family_not_present, official_duty, unknown). No use of invalid values like 'researcher' or 'inferred_from_structure'.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All evidence types are correctly assigned. Patrick's name (direct—stated in record), birthplace (direct—stated), residence (direct—stated in population schedule). Birth year ~1845 (indirect—derived from stated age by subtracting from 1850 census year; correctly not marked direct). Relationship to Thomas (indirect—inferred from household position; the 1850 census has no relationship column, so the inference is appropriate and accurately labeled). Thomas's name (direct—stated in record title and household head position). The skill correctly distinguishes between a stated fact (direct) and a computed/inferred fact (indirect). No false direct labels for inferred relationships.

output.text_response:
Reading both project files is done — now I'll fire the log entry and tree source write in parallel (neither depends on the other).

**Pre-write checklist (5d sibling stubs):**
- Subject role: `child_1` (Patrick, ~5 yrs old in 1850, in Thomas Flynn's household)
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn = **not found** in `tree.gedcomx.json` (only I1/Patrick exists) → **skip stubs**
- Siblings: none enumerable from the provided gedcomx (only P1 returned) → nothing to stubLog `log_001`, tree source `S1`. Now fire the full batch — source + 6 assertions — and simultaneously call `record_record_matches` for MXHY-TP4.The validator requires `record_persona_id: null` when there's no sidecar — since I logged this as `user_provided` (no staged ref), I need to null all persona IDs. Retrying:All persisted. Here's the summary:

---

**Extraction complete — 1850 U.S. Census, Patrick Flynn (MXHY-TP4)**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — original, FamilySearch, 1850 U.S. Federal Census
- **Assertions — `child_1` (Patrick Flynn):** 5 assertions — `a_001` name (direct), `a_002` birth ~1845 (indirect, derived from age), `a_003` birthplace Ireland (direct), `a_004` residence Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA (direct/primary), `a_005` child-of-Thomas-Flynn relationship (indirect — no 1850 relationship column)
- **Assertions — `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn):** 1 assertion — `a_006` name (direct; persona not indexed separately, `record_persona_id` null)
- **Tree changes:** S1 added; no sibling stubs (Thomas Flynn not yet in tree — no parent terminus for edges)
- **Key gap:** Birthplace is Ireland only — no county or parish. Core research objective remains open.
- **`record_persona_id` note:** All set null because the record was logged as `user_provided` (no staged sidecar); downstream `person-evidence` / `same_person` calls will need the ARK directly.

---

**FamilySearch record matches for MXHY-TP4 (informational only):**

| Confidence | Score | Record | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **4** | 0.88 | **1860 U.S. Federal Census** (MABC) | pending |
| 3 | 0.65 | Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906–1967 (MDEF) | pending |

The **1860 census match (confidence 4)** is worth pursuing next — it would show Patrick at ~15, likely still in the Flynn household, and could name Thomas Flynn with fuller household detail. The **death certificate match (confidence 3)** aligns with the project objective (death 1908, Schuylkill County) but is lower confidence; worth examining once closer to the proof conclusion.

**Suggested next steps:** run `check-warnings` for plausibility issues, then `person-evidence` to link `a_001`–`a_005` to I1, then `search-records` on the 1860 census match (MABC).

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The extraction and matching logic are factually sound, but the skill failed to persist all assertions to the tree on the first attempt due to a schema validation error on record_persona_id. The final retry succeeded, but the error reveals a conceptual inconsistency: the skill first set record_persona_id to P1 (correct for a user_provided log), then discovered the validator required null when no sidecar exists. The second batch correctly nullified all persona IDs and persisted successfully. The assertion content itself is accurate—informants are correctly identified (household member for name/age/birthplace, enumerator for residence), evidence types properly distinguish direct vs. indirect (birth year correctly marked indirect), and the relationship is appropriately inferred for an 1850 census. The record_record_matches call correctly identified both pending matches (1860 census confidence 4, death certificate confidence 3) and highlighted the higher-priority 1860 lead. No fabricated material; the gap about lacking county/parish-level birthplace is acknowledged.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: extracted six assertions (Patrick's name, birth year ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence 1850, child-of-Thomas relationship, and Thomas's name), called record_record_matches for MXHY-TP4 as instructed, reported both pending collateral matches with confidence scores, highlighted the 1860 census as the higher-priority follow-up, and added the source to the tree. The log entry was created, assertions were persisted to research.json after the retry, and the FamilySearch matches were fully documented with confidence and score details. No silent omissions of required work.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The record_record_matches call was correctly formed with id='MXHY-TP4' and matched the expected predicate. However, the initial research_append call (call #3) failed validation because all assertions carried record_persona_id='P1' despite the log entry having no sidecar (results_ref=null). The validator correctly rejected this. The retry (call #6) corrected the error by setting all record_persona_id values to null, which passed validation. This is a partial: the logical args were sound (sections, ops, entry structure), but the first attempt mishandled the schema constraint. The retry demonstrates correction, not a fundamental misunderstanding, but the initial call was indeed wrong.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the seven assertions (six for Patrick, one for Thomas) is atomic: a_001 name only, a_002 birth year only (derived, separate from the stated age that is not extracted), a_003 birthplace only, a_004 residence only, a_005 relationship only, a_006 Thomas's name only, a_007 (in final batch) Thomas's name again. No compound facts crammed into single values. Birth year ~1845 is correctly separated from the age-derivation note (which appears in informant_bias_notes, not conflated into value). The relationship assertion decomposes position in household (the observable fact) from the inference (child of Thomas) via structured_value and fact_type, not blended.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: For Patrick's name, age, and birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with informant_proximity='household_member' is accurate and specific. A ~5-year-old could not self-report; a parent must have supplied these facts. Informant_bias_notes explain the reasoning. For residence: 'census enumerator' with informant_proximity='witness' is correct—the enumerator observed where the household lived. For Thomas's name: 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with proximity='household_member' appropriately captures that an adult likely self-reported or was reported by a spouse, without over-specifying. All informant_proximity values are from the closed enum (self, witness, household_member, family_not_present, official_duty, unknown). No use of invalid values like 'researcher' or 'inferred_from_structure'.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All evidence types are correctly assigned. Patrick's name (direct—stated in record), birthplace (direct—stated), residence (direct—stated in population schedule). Birth year ~1845 (indirect—derived from stated age by subtracting from 1850 census year; correctly not marked direct). Relationship to Thomas (indirect—inferred from household position; the 1850 census has no relationship column, so the inference is appropriate and accurately labeled). Thomas's name (direct—stated in record title and household head position). The skill correctly distinguishes between a stated fact (direct) and a computed/inferred fact (indirect). No false direct labels for inferred relationships.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 11 assertions are factually correct and well-supported. The skill correctly extracted facts from the GedcomX record (Patrick's name, age 5, birth year ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence 1850; Thomas and Mary as household members with their residence). The skill properly referenced log_001 (from search-records) instead of creating a duplicate log entry. All record_persona_id values match their personas (P1 for Patrick, P2 for Thomas, P3 for Mary), and the full ARK URL was preserved in record_id. No fabricated material.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all extractable facts from the input record: Patrick's name, age, inferred birth year, birthplace, residence, and two parent-child relationships; Thomas and Mary's names and residences. The source was created and all assertions were appended. The summary output and file changes confirm all work was persisted. No major omissions or early termination.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Both MCP tool calls succeeded (matched.kind == 'live'). tree_edit call correctly added a source with proper FamilySearch URL. research_append call correctly batched 1 source + 11 assertions with proper section labels, all carrying log_entry_id: 'log_001' as required by the handoff protocol. Arguments semantically matched the task: no fixture_not_found errors, no critical ID mismatches.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Patrick's age (a_002: 'age 5') is separate from his inferred birth year (a_003: '~1845'); birthplace (a_004: 'Ireland') is separate from residence (a_005). Relationship assertions (a_006, a_007) are each one inferred link. Name assertions are separate from residence. No compound claims crammed into single values. Downstream skills can query individual fact_type values independently.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is precise. For Patrick's name/age/birthplace (a_001–a_004): 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with informant_proximity: 'household_member' and reasoning in informant_bias_notes (child ~5 could not self-report; parent likely answered). For residence (a_005): 'census enumerator' with proximity: 'witness' (correct for an observed household). Thomas and Mary similarly identified with appropriate reasoning. The skill correctly distinguished informant (who provided the data) from recorder (enumerator), and proximity aligns with the assertion type.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned. Direct evidence: Patrick's name (a_001), age 5 (a_002), birthplace Ireland (a_004), residence 1850 (a_005)—all explicitly stated in the record. Indirect evidence: birth year ~1845 (a_003)—correctly marked as inferred arithmetically from age, not directly stated. Parent-child relationships (a_006, a_007)—correctly marked indirect because the 1850 census has no relationship column; relationships are inferred from household structure by the indexer, not explicitly stated. Direct evidence assigned correctly to Thomas and Mary name/residence assertions (a_008–a_011). The skill properly distinguished stated facts from computed inferences.

| **Tree changes** | `S1` added; no person stubs — Thomas & Mary absent from tree, skip-on-no-parent condition met |
| **Key findings** | Patrick: name, age 5 (direct) + birth year ~1845 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, residence Branch Twp. Schuylkill PA 1850; Thomas Flynn and Mary Flynn named as probable parents (inferred — pre-1880 census, no relationship column); all parent-child edges classified `indirect` |
| **Next step** | Run `check-warnings` for genealogical plausibility, then `assertion-classification` to refine evidence tiers, then `person-evidence` to link Thomas & Mary to the tree |

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All 11 assertions are factually correct and well-supported. The skill correctly extracted facts from the GedcomX record (Patrick's name, age 5, birth year ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence 1850; Thomas and Mary as household members with their residence). The skill properly referenced log_001 (from search-records) instead of creating a duplicate log entry. All record_persona_id values match their personas (P1 for Patrick, P2 for Thomas, P3 for Mary), and the full ARK URL was preserved in record_id. No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all extractable facts from the input record: Patrick's name, age, inferred birth year, birthplace, residence, and two parent-child relationships; Thomas and Mary's names and residences. The source was created and all assertions were appended. The summary output and file changes confirm all work was persisted. No major omissions or early termination.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Both MCP tool calls succeeded (matched.kind == 'live'). tree_edit call correctly added a source with proper FamilySearch URL. research_append call correctly batched 1 source + 11 assertions with proper section labels, all carrying log_entry_id: 'log_001' as required by the handoff protocol. Arguments semantically matched the task: no fixture_not_found errors, no critical ID mismatches.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Patrick's age (a_002: 'age 5') is separate from his inferred birth year (a_003: '~1845'); birthplace (a_004: 'Ireland') is separate from residence (a_005). Relationship assertions (a_006, a_007) are each one inferred link. Name assertions are separate from residence. No compound claims crammed into single values. Downstream skills can query individual fact_type values independently.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is precise. For Patrick's name/age/birthplace (a_001–a_004): 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with informant_proximity: 'household_member' and reasoning in informant_bias_notes (child ~5 could not self-report; parent likely answered). For residence (a_005): 'census enumerator' with proximity: 'witness' (correct for an observed household). Thomas and Mary similarly identified with appropriate reasoning. The skill correctly distinguished informant (who provided the data) from recorder (enumerator), and proximity aligns with the assertion type.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned. Direct evidence: Patrick's name (a_001), age 5 (a_002), birthplace Ireland (a_004), residence 1850 (a_005)—all explicitly stated in the record. Indirect evidence: birth year ~1845 (a_003)—correctly marked as inferred arithmetically from age, not directly stated. Parent-child relationships (a_006, a_007)—correctly marked indirect because the 1850 census has no relationship column; relationships are inferred from household structure by the indexer, not explicitly stated. Direct evidence assigned correctly to Thomas and Mary name/residence assertions (a_008–a_011). The skill properly distinguished stated facts from computed inferences.
